title: H&R Block’s AI Pivot Weighs On Valuation And Momentum Concerns
content:
Track your investments for FREE with Simply Wall St, the portfolio command center trusted by over 7 million individual investors worldwide.

H&R Block (NYSE:HRB) is shifting from a seasonal tax preparation model to a year round, AI-driven financial services platform. New CEO Curtis Campbell is rolling out AI Tax Assist and other tools intended to automate workflows and support advisory services. The company is positioning its technology to keep clients engaged beyond the core tax season.

For investors watching H&R Block at a share price of $29.8, this pivot comes after a mixed performance profile. The stock shows a 59.5% gain over five years, while returns over 1 year and year to date reflect declines of 47.6% and 30.1% respectively, with a 7.4% decline over the past week. Those numbers frame how meaningful any change in the underlying business model could be for sentiment around NYSE:HRB.

The move toward an AI supported, year round platform signals that H&R Block is aiming to widen its role in customers’ financial lives, not just at tax time. As these tools roll out and the service mix evolves, investors can track how consistently clients use H&R Block’s offerings across the year and how that usage may influence the company’s positioning in tax and adjacent financial services.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for H&R Block by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on H&R Block.NYSE:HRB Earnings & Revenue Growth as at Apr 2026

4 things going right for H&R Block that this headline doesn't cover.

Quick Assessment

✅ Price vs Analyst Target: At US$29.80, H&R Block trades about 27% below the US$41.00 analyst target. ✅ Simply Wall St Valuation: Shares are flagged as trading roughly 60.2% below an estimated fair value. ❌ Recent Momentum: The 30 day return of about 2.2% decline shows sentiment has been weak ahead of this AI driven shift.

There's only one way to assess whether it may be the right time to buy, sell or hold H&R Block. Head to Simply Wall St's company report for the latest analysis of H&R Block's fair value.

Key Considerations

📊 The move to an AI supported, year round platform could change how much of H&R Block's revenue depends on the traditional tax season over time. 📊 Watch adoption of AI Tax Assist, client retention outside tax season and whether margins hold up as technology spending and advisory services scale. ⚠️ The major red flag is negative shareholders' equity, which makes balance sheet strength crucial as the company invests in new technology.

Story Continues

Dig Deeper

For the full picture including more risks and rewards, check out the complete H&R Block analysis. Alternatively, you can review the community page for H&R Block to see how other investors believe this latest news will impact the company's narrative.

This article by Simply Wall St is general in nature. We provide commentary based on historical data and analyst forecasts only using an unbiased methodology and our articles are not intended to be financial advice. It does not constitute a recommendation to buy or sell any stock, and does not take account of your objectives, or your financial situation. We aim to bring you long-term focused analysis driven by fundamental data. Note that our analysis may not factor in the latest price-sensitive company announcements or qualitative material. Simply Wall St has no position in any stocks mentioned.

Companies discussed in this article include HRB.

Have feedback on this article? Concerned about the content? Get in touch with us directly. Alternatively, email editorial-team@simplywallst.com

title: Self-employment taxes: what every freelancer should know
content:
Host of The Big Idea, Elizabeth Gore unpacks the basics of self-employment tax from the 15.3% rate to who’s required to file and how it’s reported. Gore also explains key details like Schedule SE, deductions, and when quarterly estimated payments come into play, giving entrepreneurs a straightforward roadmap to manage their tax obligations with confidence

Video Transcript

00:05 Speaker A

Welcome back to the big idea presented by Block advisors built by H&R Block.

00:10 Speaker A

With tax season in full swing, I want to take a second to break down estimated tax payments for small business owners.

00:16 Speaker A

As a small business owner, you likely will not have income tax and other payroll taxes withheld from your earnings like an employee would.

00:25 Speaker A

That's where estimated tax payments come in.

00:27 Speaker A

They are designed to make sure you are paying taxes periodically during the year instead of all at once cuz that's too much.

00:35 Speaker A

Most small business owners use form 1040 ES to figure out their estimated payments. The good news is, you can submit all of that online.

00:43 Speaker A

These payments typically cover income tax and self-employed tax.

00:48 Speaker A

Self-employment taxes are basically your social security and Medicare contributions similar to what employees have withheld from their wages.

00:54 Speaker A

So, when are you required to make these payments?

00:58 Speaker A

Generally, you'll need to if you expect to owe a thousand or more in taxes.

1:02 Speaker A

Now, that's income tax plus self-employment tax.

1:04 Speaker A

And your withholding doesn't cover 90% of this year's tax or 100% of last year's tax.

1:11 Speaker A

It's also important to consider all your sources of income when estimating how much you'll owe.

1:17 Speaker A

Estimated payments are due on a quarterly schedule.

1:21 Speaker A

April 15th for Q1, June 15th for Q2, September 15th for Q3, and don't forget January 15th of the following year for Q4.

1:29 Speaker A

Making these payments on time is really important y'all because there's a penalty for missing them.

1:35 Speaker A

It's not a flat fee like many payments you might be used to, but it's calculated for each quarter you miss. so put it on your calendar.

1:44 Speaker A

The IRS determines that penalty by taking the federal short-term rate for the quarter you missed and adding 3%.

1:52 Speaker A

One last thing now, if your business is taxed as a C corporation, the rules are a little different.

1:57 Speaker A

The corporation itself pays income tax and has to make its own estimated payments.

2:00 Speaker A

So, make sure you understand the requirements for your business type.

title: AG1 CEO Kat Cole on turning customer feedback into growth
content:
AG1 CEO, Kat Cole, sits down with Host, Elizabeth Gore, to explore how every job, no matter how small, can prepare you to build and lead a company. Cole shares lessons from her rise through roles at Hooters and beyond, emphasizing the power of staying close to customers, focusing on one great product, and learning from mistakes. The conversation also dives into leadership, decision-making, health habits, and practical strategies entrepreneurs can use to grow both their businesses and themselves.

Video Transcript

0:00 spk_0

Now, I bet you didn't start your business to do taxes. Good thing Block Advisors did. Block Advisors by H&R Block is your one-stop shop for small business support, taxes, bookkeeping, payroll, and more. Join the more than 2 million small business owners who trust our experts year after year.Let us handle the details so you can focus on what you do best. Visit block advisors.com.

0:24 spk_1

I'm in the business. I'm navigating my own decisions, the team that I've built, and still it was time to make changes, to drive more science, more speed, more innovation, and that could only happen by talking to multiple customers every week, making sure the language they were using and the products they wanted us to make were reflected in our innovation.

0:52 spk_0

Howdy y'all. I'm Elizabeth Gore. Welcome to the Big Idea from Yahoo Finance presented by Block Advisors built by H&R Block. As the co-founder of the small business funding platform, Hello Wallace, it has always been my mission to help ensure entrepreneurs have the tools they need to succeed. So let's cowboy up.Today's big idea question is, how does every job prep you to launch your own company? And our industry focus is wellness supplements. The US dietary supplement market was about worth $68 billion in 2025. That's probably half me taking them. And it's projected to reach $131 billion by 2033. That means this is one of the fastest growing.Health segments for consumer wellness. Joining us to talk about this today is Kat Cole, CEO of AG1. OK, so what does Cinnabon and Hooters have in common? Reminiate on that. It is my guest, Kat Cole. She took both of these companies and turned them into empires in her own tenure. Let's get to our conversation. I'm super excited to welcome Kat to the Big Idea.Cat Cole, welcome to the Big Idea.How long have we known each other?Pre-kids

2:09 spk_1

tryingto think about it easy pre-kids, um, Fortune and PW entrepreneur, I mean, 1415 years,

2:20 spk_0

yeah, 3 careers, I think within that babies, husbands, you got, you got a husband sailing around the world, rowing around the world right now. It's so good to have you on.Um, I've, I've loved you for so long. We, we seek each other out, I think, at every event to catch up, and I, I think one of the reasons I was so excited to have you on today is, is your spirit and leadership has such a softness, yet there's so much grit way inside your belly.And I think a big part of that, I mean, you, you worked your booty off from when you were a kid. I mean, you, you had tough growing up, abuse, tough environments, had to contribute back to your, your mom, your sister, you know, what, what does, what does that create in, in a CEO?

3:15 spk_1

There are a few things that type of upbringing creates in the CEO. One is calm under pressure, you know, when things get busy and hectic, uh, I, whether it's nature or nurture, likely both, I get more calm.And I've heard, I heard someone say once that, uh, a CEO who's stepping into their role properly actually isn't making a lot of decisions. The team is, but the few that get, but the few that get to the CEO are obviously the ones that a great team still needs that extra layer of decision.Making on. And so, essentially, I get the best of the best and the worst of the worst situations and decisions, and, uh, it's a privilege and a responsibility. And so I think that upbringing builds a good muscle for that. Um, it also builds a muscle for getting really clear about whose opinion I care. So, do I care about the family members who aren't living life in a way that my mom and I.Uh, believed we're not aligned with our values? No. Um, do I care about people who have an opinion about how I'm working or what I'm building, if they're not in it with me? No, I've also heard the phrase, the people who might be criticizing you or tearing you down are often not also in the trenches like you are like real.don't have pot. Uh, so yeah,

doesn't itsound exhausting to take other people down? I don't have time for that crap. It's a lot of work. I was like I'm exhausted.

So many great. I have so many great customers and so much incredible work, right, so much great innovation, and

I don'thave time for the people I like right now, much less the people I don't. Lord, well, your, your storied career started at Hooters, and you know, one of the things that is fascinating about you is you did every stinking job.Both on site and then incorporate. Yeah,yeah, started as a hostess, then a waitress, then started training new employees, worked every job in the restaurant, just so happened that company that I was an hourly employee for was growing.And there are more opportunities in a company that grows than in a company that does not. So I had opportunities to take expanded leadership roles when I was 18 and 19 years old, travel around the world when I was 19 and 20, opening franchises, join the corporate office at 20, vice president at 26, grow with the company.Uh, you know, doing 8 900 million in annual sales, and it was incredible, and I, I learned so much. I did not have the foresight or the ambition.To become a CEO in that industry, I was trying to pay my bills so I could go to college and finish my engineering degree and go to law school. Like that was theThat was the dream. And, and, and yet this, uh, inner drive to support myself led to working every job, which led to a resume that made me qualified for more opportunities. And so the work continued and then the opportunities came, and then the work expanded and more opportunities came. Same thing at Cinnabon when I joined the company in 2010 as.President and at the heart of the Great Recession, I did almost nothing but work in the bakeries, drive trucks with delivery teams, spend time with hourly workers to understand the business at the most fundamental level, so I could make the fastest and most effective decisions while building relationships with the people who my decisions affected the most. Same thing with AG1.Uh, when I joined AG1, uh, spent so much time talking to customers who were making their subscription, getting their delivery, spending time in our blending facilities, understanding what's going on.Uh, in the quality controls and at every step, getting close to the action, close to the business, from a quality production and a customer experience standpoint. And it has the same benefit and outcome. I understand the business more effectively. I have more respect with the people who do those roles, and my decisions can be faster and more.Effective because they're grounded in the reality of the business.

I, I'm just curious if you think the future of being a leader are the ones who keep digging into the ground truth. I'm just curious about that.

Absolutely, it is. I, I remember when I stepped in as CEO of AG1 a few years after joining as president and COO, we had a few very clear, um, directives and ambitions. One, innovate the product and continue to upgrade it and lead in the area of science and human clinical trials, which was unheard of in the industry.To expand products, so radically accelerate innovation. We built this business off one product in one channel to half a billion in revenue. It's insane. And so moving it at that scale to something different, just a few more products, just a few more channels, is a big shift for the company.And this idea of expanding channels going into retail, which we did, adding new products like AGZ and flavors, all that change in an established business requires three things. It requires trust amongst the team, which only comes from staying close to the true truth. It requires having a bias for speed, which can only be done if you know the true truth. If you're responding toUm, delusional data or, uh, hearing

things that say that again, delusional data,

delusional data which could come from hallucinations.

I like

that from, uh, AI or it could come from, it could come from a culture where people onlytell

you what emotions, yeah, absolutely. Oh, I like that delusional data, whether, whether that's with my own children or my employees. I'm using that.

So, this idea of effective decision making for speed can only happen if you have, uh, and I've used a phrase very similar to what you just did, the true truth. I'd say I want to know the true truth, not the truth you want me to hear, not the truth you think I want to know, but the true truth. If people know it and think it and feel it, it's gonna save me a lot of time if I just get to it right away. So that's the second thing.Uh, and then the third is having that bias for action, which can only come from having confidence and relationships, from staying close to the action, that true truth. That is what allowed even a few years into AG1. Now I'm not new and curious. I'm in the business, I'm navigating my own decisions, the team that I've built, and still it was time to make changes to drive more science, more speed, more innovation.And that could only happen by talking to multiple customers every week, making sure the language they were using and the products they wanted us to make were reflected in our innovation.

It's so interesting.

It looks like they're uncomfortable, or, yeah, you know, can't get around it, and it is so important, whether it was when I was, um, we took bought Jamba Juice, took it private from being a publicly traded company when I was COO of Focus Brands, and had this vision to help it stay what it is, but be more healthy, it lost its way a little, and that requiredDrinking a lot of smoothies and going, going into so many locations, and, and, and again, it's, it's what you're saying, it is both the real human experience that using your own product gives you, but it is also the respect.That you earn from those who are doing the work and customers customers can tell if it's performative or if it's real. I'm regularly uploading videos of me making my EG1 or traveling with it on planes, and one, I'm proud and I want to show that off, and I want.That occasion to be in people's feeds, but it's also to find connection with other people who are doing the same with our community, with our customers and people. that's right, and uhAnd so thiscumulative body of answers gives me a pattern of something I could stop doing that would save time, save resources, and not negatively affect the company. I used these 3 questions when I was turning around Cinnabon in the recession, when I was, uh, evolving Jamba Juice and Moe's Southwest Grill to be more health forward as the consumer moved many years later, um, to put to document and or to innovate and put technology in place before it was obvious that we needed it in those franchise businesses, and certainly,At AG1. You have human clinical trials behind your product. Can you make more things that I'm already buying, but I don't trust as much? They would just add a scoop of something.Shake it up and making their AG1 even more one comprehensive. Stress is a lot of what keeps us from winding down, getting asleep, and staying asleep, or if we wake up.Uh, which happens a lot in certain age groups at 2:00 a.m., 30 a.m., have the ability to get back to sleep without knocking ourselves out, not being able to hear our kids when we want to and need to, or feeling groggy in the morning. Our customers were already making their own version of this sleepy girl mocktail with magnesium, ashwagandda, L-theanine, and herbal sleep support, so wePut that all together in one different, better NSF for sport, third party certified, boom, customer insights driven. 23:35 spk_0

I'm gonna stop you there because you're talking to a full-blown perimenopausal woman with kids who is exhausted, wakes up in the middle of the night and pees too much.So you just put my entire health map into a product, um, of which I will be snorting appropriately so I can sleep good.Oh my God, I'll just, just tape it all over me, be like, uh.No, it's exciting, right? Oh, great question. So,When I joined the company, it had been bootstrapped. The founder had bootstrapped the business zero outside capital to 100 almost 160 million in revenue. Unheard of. That is unheard of. Holy moly. He was a, but he was an excellent capital allocator, right? One, the product has healthy margins to fund the business. Two,He wasn't obsessed with percentages. He was cash, customers, and inventory, and so this idea of if I have the cash, and I have the inventory, and I have the customer interest, although it may cost me more to make product more quickly, ship it more quickly, turning that inventory into cash is the most important thing.And then having customers who have such a great experience that I don't have to keep marketing to new customers as much because those existing customers tell their friends. So, in that effort to turn inventory into cash and revenue, I could never compromise on quality and taste because that is what drives personal referral, which lowers my marketing costs. So that was the foundation. The roots of the business are very bootstrapped, self-fund, butThen we did raise um a really healthy round, 120 million. Uh, the business was valued at 1.3 billion post that fundraise. This was at the end of 21, beginning of 22, and that was our big growth round. So that bolstered our balance sheet.And we used that to fund a bit of the growth because there were so many health tailwinds from COVID that it made sense to keep leaning into that growth. 2, to build a team of PhDs, set up the US supply chain. The product had been blended only in New Zealand previously, and we wanted to stand up US manufacturing. This was many years pre-tariffs, so we were not, uh, we were not psychic. It was just the right thing to do.And, but it benefited us when, you know, when, when the tariffs came.And, uh, and so that mindset of take the growth capital, use it to fund some growth, but use it to fund in the near term, but use most of it to fund capabilities that will build long term growth, better local supply chain, uh, better QA, faster inventory management and innovation capability, our own lab, these types for prototyping, right? These types of things allowed us to not onlyMove AG1 more quickly and grow more quickly to your point of when it was time to make AGZ, we had the internal capacity. I had the domestic blending, uh, uh, capability. I owned the sourcing, so I could just still use my team to go get.Chamomile and epi and uh uh glycine and myoanosheol and uh Ashwagandha because I already had the team in the supply chain set up and we could use now our operating cash flows because we became profitable while we were growing to fund our own innovation.So we have the balance sheet between our fundraise, what has been now almost 5 years ago, and our operating cash flows to fund innovation likethis.

27:28 spk_0

All right, Kat, hold that thought. We will be right back here on the Big Idea.If you are an employee, you generally have payroll taxes withheld from your wages, including tips by your employer. But if you are self-employed, this does not happen.That's where self-employment taxes come into play. So what is self-employment tax? Self-employment tax is equal to 15.3% of your earnings from self-employment, and it represents payroll taxes, i.e., the employer and employee share 12.4% for Social Security taxes and 2.9% for Medicare taxes.For employees, they only pay half or 7.65% of the total payroll taxes owned and the employer pays the other half. On the other hand, self-employed individuals must account for the full 15.3%. But fear not, you can generally deduct half of the self-employment tax you pay on your individual tax return. How do you determine self-employment tax and where is it reported?If you have over 400 in earnings from self-employment, you are required to file Schedule SE with your individual return at tax time. You will use the schedule to calculate the amount of self-employment tax owed, which is then added to the income tax on your individual return. Note that only sole proprietor, partners and members of an LLC taxed as either of those will be required to pay self-employment tax on their share of the business earnings.S corporation shareholders, on the other hand, do not have to pay self-employment tax on their share of the business earnings. There is an additional 0.9% Medicare tax if your self-employment earnings exceed the threshold amount for your filing status. Can I pay self-employment taxes periodically during the year?Yes. In fact, if you expect to owe at least $1000 in total tax for the year, i.e., your income and self-employment taxes combined, you may be required to make estimated payments. You generally make estimated payments on a quarterly basis, and a tax professional can determine if you are required to make them and how much you should pay. Now, let's jump back into our conversation with Cat Cole.SoWhen I look at you, I just see a success in your DNA and all your experience, and I'm curious about, um, we have our dirty unicorn that we talk about on The Big Idea. I'm curious about what's, what's a big mistake in business that you've made that we can all learn from. What's your dirty unicorn?

30:20 spk_1

There's, um, there's a big one, and probably it, I think it's the biggest mistake I've ever made as a leader. So when I was, um, the president of Cinnabon, we, through asking these questions that we've talked about, I heard from our team members that one of the solutions needed in the business was a smaller portion. Cinnabon was known for, at the time, cinnamon rolls the size of your face and no other variety.And yet we had not launched a smaller portion, which was a clear solution to many problems. One problem was affordability. A smaller thing can be cheaper than a bigger thing. Another problem was responsible indulgence. People, you know, we're starting to actually eat the way they answered surveys. Before it was, I want to be healthy, but I'm not making those choices. In 2010 and 2011, that shifted. People actually started preferring smaller treats when they were.Indulging. They were looking for, uh, to be more calorie conscious when they indulged. And so while no one was going to Cinnabon for their diet, it was still a huge opportunity to have a smaller product. A smaller product's also more portable at a time where people are more on the go, not sitting down as much, andWe launched a smaller product, and it was a huge success in the franchise business. So I had all this trust from the franchisees, and then it was time to keep pushing the growth boundaries by putting product in other locations, grocery stores, fresh bakeries, because we only had 30-400 mall locations, um, in the US. We were franchised around the world, a few 100 million in annual revenue, and, uh, we had this opportunity.To grow the business through consumer products, and we had this incredible opportunity to put versions of our product, uh, in grocery stores and in bakery chains, and one was Costco, incredible bakers, great big fresh bakery, and the short version of a long story is it was supposed to be a product that was very differentiated from our franchise product, and it ended up being almost exactlyWhat we sold in the franchise business, but, uh, for half the price, and my franchisees felt, um, lied to, and it immediately torpedoed trust. I like to say outside of losing life and losing love, there's nothing worse than losing trust. And, uh, what happened was the leader of the sales team.Sent the recipe for the big roles because the buyer there said that's what they wanted without checking in, and we didn't have the processes in place to catch that a decision could be made that quickly. And so really it was on me as a leader for not putting the right systems in place. I couldn't have predicted what would go wrong in a new channel, but I should have predicted something could because things could move so quickly in such a capable partner.And I had to walk away from the deal and pull the product out of Costco. It was incredibly expensive, incredibly painful, but I also had to demonstrate to my key stakeholder, my franchisees, that while I had the right to do this, the legal right, and the product was incredibly successful there, it wasn't handled right. Just because you have the right to do something does not remove your responsibility to need to handle it correctly and to honor the core of your business.And so I went to them and said, OK, we're gonna pull out of this, uh, of this opportunity, not because you're whining, not because you're upset, because I failed to ensure it was communicated and handled properly. That built trust back.Three months later, we had the opportunity to put the mini bon, the smaller cinnamon roll that we had implemented that literally saved the business after the recession into 7000 Burger Kings. And when I went to the franchisees, they said, we don't love it because, you know, it's scary for them to have their products.Of course, they said, we don't love it, but because of how you handled Costco, we trust you. And that relationship single-handedly tripled, tripled the business in a year, benefited the franchisees, benefited the company. We did a revenue share with the franchisees. It was transformative, but only possible on the heels of making a huge mistake, owning it, fixing it, and putting systems in place to do it better next time.

title: H&R Block wants to be more than a tax company. It wants to be your year-round financial advisor
content:
Curtis Campbell knows exactly what kind of company people think H&R Block is.

It is the place you go in late winter or early spring when your W-2s, 1099s, deductions, and deadlines are piling up, your refund feels urgent, and your anxiety is rising. It is familiar, local, and useful, but not the kind of company that usually comes to mind when people think about product velocity, AI deployment, or a culture of experimentation.

Campbell wants to change that.

The new CEO, who previously served as H&R Block’s president and chief product officer, is trying to turn the 70-year-old tax preparation giant into something more expansive: a year-round financial platform powered by software, data, and AI, while preserving the human trust that has long set the company apart. That means reimagining H&R Block not just as a seasonal tax brand, but as a business that can advise creators, serve small businesses, help customers manage money, and use technology to automate the most tedious parts of tax prep, so employees can focus on judgment, relationships, and guidance.

For Campbell, this is an operational exercise rather than a branding one. “Fundamentally, we do taxes, but we’re really not in the tax business,” he tells Fortune. “We’re in the business of trust and confidence.”

Campbell is leading H&R Block through a volatile tax period, as sweeping tax-law changes collide with a significantly downsized IRS. What he hears from the field is that clients want help understanding what the changes mean and reassurance that they are not missing out on money or making mistakes.

That urgency could benefit assisted tax preparation in the near term. But Campbell’s ambition reaches well past one potentially chaotic filing season.

More than a tax company

Campbell’s core thesis is that H&R Block should no longer think of itself as a company that shows up for customers once a year. He wants it to be present throughout the year, serving customers on their journey toward financial freedom.

Such a pivot requires looking beyond tax preparation, he says, to adjacent services such as payroll, bookkeeping, tax advisory, small-business support, and banking. It also means using the company’s enormous dataset and physical footprint to build continuous relationships with customers.

Campbell describes a future in which H&R Block becomes more embedded in clients’ lives, moving from “a once-a-year engagement” to “a multiyear engagement.”

He sees a strong opening in the growing ranks of independent workers, creators, and influencers who are suddenly operating as small businesses without fully understanding the financial infrastructure that comes with that shift. H&R Block launched a Creator Suite earlier this year, bundling tax help with bookkeeping, payroll, and related services for people whose work may span multiple income streams and business entities.

Story Continues

“That’s a really interesting group for us to target,” Campbell says of creators. “They’re folks whose tax situation becomes more complex over time, so they’re more needy because they’re advancing their business, they’re growing their sort of portfolio of different services and offerings.”

The same logic applies to small businesses, which Campbell calls H&R Block’s fastest-growing business and one he believes Wall Street still undervalues. The opportunity is straightforward: Small-business owners do not want to spend their time on payroll, bookkeeping, and tax compliance. They want to run their businesses.

“I’ve yet to meet a small-business owner who loves taxes or payroll or bookkeeping,” he says. “They just love the thing that they do.”

The AWS playbook comes to tax prep

What makes Campbell an intriguing fit for this moment is that he does not fit the stereotype of a tax executive. He is a Southern-born tech leader who spent years at Dell and AWS, companies known for fast-moving product cycles and quick execution. He talks in the language of critical assumptions, hypotheses, road maps, and experiments. He sounds more like a cloud executive than the steward of a legacy tax brand. That is partly the point.

Campbell is trying to make H&R Block more technologically fluent, not just in what it sells but in how it operates. He says the company has spent considerable time defining its “ideal state,” mapping the assumptions required to get there, and building a long-term road map. From there, teams identify the questions they need to answer and the experiments required to answer them. Its pace has picked up sharply in recent years.

“Before I joined H&R Block, on average, we ran about five experiments a year,” he says. “This year, we’re going to average about 123.”

That shift is partly cultural. Campbell has pushed H&R Block to act more like a learning organization, drawing on a lesson from Amazon: The companies that build the fastest feedback loops are often the ones best positioned to win over time.

That has also required changing the company’s approach to failure. In the past, new ideas often carried a stigma if they did not work. Campbell wants teams to see failed tests as useful if they produce insight. In his view, a good experiment creates learning, whether it ends in a win or a loss.

That mentality is straight out of Big Tech. But tax preparation is not cloud software, and Campbell is quick to acknowledge that speed has to be handled differently in a business where mistakes carry real consequences.

“You don’t have to risk the business to test an idea,” he says. “You could say, ‘Hey, I’ve got a concept that’s X, Y, and Z. How can I test this within 10 customers to gain knowledge?’ If that proves to be successful, you can then start to scale it.”

Building an AI tax company

The most visible expression of H&R Block’s transformation is its use of AI.

Campbell is in an unusual position here. Unlike many CEOs who inherit someone else’s technology stack, he helped build the systems now being stress-tested under his leadership.

One of those tools is AI Tax Assist, built into H&R Block’s DIY product in partnership with OpenAI. Campbell describes it as an on-demand guide for customers who get confused while filing online and need quick answers. Users can ask whether an expense qualifies as a deduction or what a move might mean for their taxes, and the system responds in real time.

He recently tried it on his upcoming move from Dallas to Kansas City, asking about the tax implications of going from Texas, which has no state income tax, to Missouri, which does.

The company has also developed an AI assistant for tax professionals, code-named Sidekick, built directly into the software H&R Block’s preparers use every day. Campbell says tax pros have responded enthusiastically because the tool lets them ask highly specific questions and get answers grounded in the company’s own tax institute, an internal bench of enrolled agents, CPAs, and tax attorneys who monitor regulatory changes and work closely with the IRS and state authorities.

Campbell argues that AI can make H&R Block more tech-forward without stripping out the human element that customers value most. He divides tax prep into two kinds of work: the mechanical tasks of collecting information, entering it, and calculating outcomes, and the relational work of advising clients and earning their trust.

He bets that AI can take on more of the first category, freeing tax professionals to spend more time on the second. Today, he says, most office time still goes to transactional work. His ideal model flips that, with technology handling much of the back-end processing, so client conversations become the focus.

H&R Block is already testing that approach in five offices. “At the end of the day, [tax pros] view their success based on their client’s success. If we enable them to do more of that, they’re going to feel more fulfilled.”

Becoming a year-round financial platform

H&R Block’s innovation push extends well beyond tax prep. Campbell’s larger goal is to use software, data, and AI to build the company into a year-round financial platform with a deeper role in customers’ lives. As he sees it, financial pain points do not fit neatly into a filing deadline or a single season.

Spruce, the company’s mobile banking product, is one example. Campbell frames it as a logical extension of H&R Block’s expanding suite of financial services, part of a broader effort to remain relevant to customers year-round.

He also believes H&R Block’s footprint of roughly 9,000 offices still matters in a digital future. As the company becomes more virtual, Campbell expects more customers to engage with tax pros through software and mobile devices. But he sees the storefronts as a key part of H&R Block’s omnichannel advantage, especially in communities where trust is still built face-to-face. “It is important for us to be where our clients are,” he says.

That line captures the core of Campbell’s strategy. He is trying to modernize H&R Block without losing its local presence, human expertise, and credibility within the tax space.

The challenge is persuading the market to see H&R Block as all of those things at once: a techie tax company with thousands of offices; a compliance-heavy business trying to move faster and experiment more; a human service brand adopting AI aggressively; and a seasonal name aiming to become a year-round financial platform.

Campbell seems comfortable with that tension. If he succeeds, H&R Block’s next chapter will be about more than improving tax prep. It will be about turning the company into a more innovative business with a deeper presence in Americans’ financial lives.

This story was originally featured on Fortune.com

View Comments

title: Nearly Half of Working Canadians (46%) Say They’re as Private About Their Income as Their Nudity, Reveals New H&R Block Canada Survey
content:
H&R Block Canada Inc.

63% say they’d work harder if they were paid better despite 60% getting a pay increase; H&R Block points to the tax credits and benefits that drive the biggest income tax refunds

CALGARY, Alberta, April 07, 2026 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) -- A new survey by H&R Block Canada reveals that while most working Canadians love their job, many feel cynical around their pay level and today’s realities around pay increases. While 60% of working Canadians received an increase in the last 12 months, 27% say they were disappointed or offended by the increase amount, which averaged 4.3%. The survey revealed that, on average, Canadians believe nearly $85,000 is the income needed for a single adult to get by and put a little aside into savings or for an emergency fund. However, this amount differs across regions with British Columbia being the highest at nearly $93,000, followed by Alberta ($89,000), Ontario ($87,000), Saskatchewan and Manitoba ($82,000), Atlantic Canada and Quebec ($76,000).

“We can’t help with pay increases – but we can help put money back into Canadians’ pockets,” said Yannick Lemay, Tax Expert at H&R Block Canada. “While there’re hundreds of tax credits and benefits, the largest refunds often come from a combination of family benefits, such as the Canada Child Benefit, refundable credits like the Canadian Workers Benefit, and the Canada Groceries and Essentials Benefit, and major deductions, including for RRSP contributions or employment expenses. The good news is that last year, we helped around 70% of Canadians get a refund. We hope to help even more Canadians get a refund this tax season.”

2026 H&R Block Survey - Key Findings Among Working Canadians:

Nearly half of working Canadians (46%) are as private about their income as they are their nudity: Despite feeling wildly private about others knowing their salary, many working Canadians are keen to know the salaries of their peers - with 44% saying they try to find out what their peers are earning to asses if their own income is fair or not.

Canadians have polarizing views when it comes to loving their job: Nearly two-thirds (64%) of working Canadians say they love their job. This is despite 71% of Canadians who feel employers have less focus on work-life balance in today’s reality, and 50% of those working who report feeling burnt out in their job. Regardless of their career path, 58% say they sometimes have job remorse, regretting that they didn’t take a different career path.

Canadians are divided on how they feel about pay: Working Canadians have conflicting views about their current pay level, with 51% who feel they’re fairly paid by their employer versus 49% who feel underpaid. Overall, 70% believe their income isn’t reflective of today’s high costs of living. More than a third (35%) of Canadians say they can’t stretch their paycheque to see them through to their next paycheque, often needing to use credit cards, overdrafts, and bridging loans until their next pay day. The survey reveals this is a bigger issue for Canadians in Alberta, Saskatchewan and Manitoba at 42%, followed by Quebec (39%), Atlantic Canada (36%), Ontario (31%), and British Columbia (28%).

Story Continues

More than half of working Canadians say they’d work harder if they were paid more: Overall, 63% say if they were paid more they’d be more motivated to work harder. Over two in five (44%) say their frustration with their pay makes them less willing to work harder. This sentiment is more prevalent among younger Canadians at 47% among 18-34 year olds), compared to 38% among those aged 35-54, and 27% for 55+.

Four in ten working Canadians feel undervalued by their pay level; nearly one third say they’ll look for other work if they don’t get an increase: More than 4 in 10 Canadian (43%) say their salary makes them feel undervalued by their employer; 30% say unless they get a pay increase they’ll look for a new job in the next 12 months (with those aged 18-34 being the most motivated to switch jobs for more pay (39%) followed by 35-54 year-olds (29%), and just 16% of Canadians 55+).

Six in ten working Canadians received a pay increase in the last 12 months; 29% are not holding their breath for one: Among Canadians currently working, 60% received a pay rise in the last 12 months; 49% received an increase without a promotion and 11% received an increased as part of a promotion. Nearly a third of Canadians (29%) haven’t received an increase and don’t expect one in the next 6 months.

Average pay increase was 4.3% with men receiving significantly higher average increases than women: Among Canadians that received an increase, the overall mean average pay increase was 4.3%. Canadians who identify as male received an average 4.9% increase, compared to Canadians who identify as female at 3.7%.

More than 1 in 4 Canadians (27%) felt disappointed or even offended by their pay increase: Overall, 35% of those that received an increase say they felt happy with the amount, 38% felt neutral. Conversely, 21% were disappointed it wasn’t higher and 6% were offended by the increase amount. Nearly half of working Canadians (45%) say they’d be willing to do a job they dislike if it meant earning more money.

Canadians are cynical about employers’ rationale for pay increases – or lack of: A whopping 90% of Canadians say employers often use the excuse of it being a challenging economic time to justify not providing pay increases. Overall, 74% think annual increases are no longer the norm, with 83% who say employers don’t give pay increases as much in today’s reality due to their focus on profits versus retaining employees long-term. Two-thirds of Canadians (67%) say employers are often biased in giving increases to employees they like the most.

H&R Block Canada Points to Key Tax Credits, Benefits and Deductions That Often Drive the Biggest Refunds:

Canada Child Benefit (CCB): One of the largest government payments, which can only be accessed through filing your taxes for the current year, provides tax-free payments to eligible families up to $7,997 per child, $6,748 per child between 6-17, and up to an additional $3,411 for a child with a disability, per year.  Canada Groceries and Essentials Benefit (formerly the GST/HST Credit): A quarterly credit payment for eligible couples or single Canadians based on household income, to help offset the cost of sales tax. Payments are up to around $700 for single adults, and up to around $1,400 for a family of four including the one-time payment being made this spring, but can only be received with a current tax return.  Canada Workers Benefit (CWB): A refundable tax credit for modest-income individuals and families based on adjusted family net income, for up to $1,633 for single Canadians and $2,813 for families, and supplement of up to $843 for people with a disability. Amounts vary in Alberta, Nunavut and Quebec.  Disability Tax Credit (DTC): Up to $10,138 for adults with a disability or supporting family members, and up to $16,052 for children under 18, with a $5,914 child disability supplement.  Tuition and Education Credits: A credit of up to 14.5% of eligible fees and student loan interest. If recipients don’t claim the full amount, they can carry the credit forward for future use or transfer up to $5,000 to a family member.  Medical Expense Tax Credit: Covers the cost of multiple medical related expenses (prescriptions, dental, fertility treatments, etc.) above a threshold of the lesser of $2,834 in 2025 - or 3% of your net income.  Child Care Expense Deduction: Parents can deduct childcare costs incurred to work or attend school, up to $8,000 per child under 7, $5,000 per child aged 7-16, and $11,000 for children eligible for the Disability Tax Credit.  RRSP Contributions: Canadians accumulate contribution rights up to 18% of earned income or a max of $32,490 (whichever is lower) per year to reduce taxable income. Contributing to RRSPs can also lead to large refunds when contributions push income into lower tax brackets.

About the survey 
These findings are from a survey conducted by H&R Block from Feb. 19-23, 2026, among a representative sample of 1545 Canadians who are members of the Angus Reid Forum. For comparison purposes only, a probability sample of this size would carry a margin of error of +/-2.53 percentage points, 19 times out of 20. In some cases, data is rebased to exclude respondents the question did not apply to.

About H&R Block Canada: A trusted partner of Canadians for over 60 years, H&R Block Canada the market leader in assisted tax preparation. Serving almost 1,000 locations across the country that includes a network of Canadian franchise business owners, H&R Block's team of Tax Experts use the latest in technological advances combined with real-world expertise to help people file taxes in office, file remotely, or use our award-winning tax software, named moneyGenius.ca’s Best Tax Software two years in a row. H&R Block Canada can support in the preparation of personal, small business, corporate, U.S., rental, and estate taxes. H&R Block's comprehensive education program, Tax Academy, ensures our Tax Experts continually update their skills. Learn more at www.hrblock.ca or 1-800-HRBLOCK.

For more information, contact: H&R Block c/o Ketchum: hrblockmediainquiries@ketchum.com

title: CEOs are lining up behind the $1,000 Trump Accounts for babies
content:
In today’s CEO Daily: Diane Brady examines why so many companies are eager to contribute to Trump Accounts. The big leadership story: H&R Block’s new CEO on what distinguishes C-suite leaders from middle management. The markets: Mostly up amid reports that the U.S. and Iran are considering a ceasefire. Plus: All the news and watercooler chat from Fortune.

Good morning. The Treasury Department announced yesterday that BNY and Robinhood will build and run the app for Trump’s tax-deferred investing accounts for kids, which is due to launch in July and be seeded with $1,000 of federal money for babies born between 2025 and 2028. While critics say there are better places to deploy that cash, investing early is a time-tested way to build wealth. That may be why companies like Nvidia, JPMorgan Chase, BlackRock, Intel, Citigroup, Chipotle, Delta Air Lines, and Coinbase have pledged to match the Treasury grant for employees’ children. It’s why Dell Technologies CEO Michael Dell and his wife Susan stepped up to donate $6.25 billion to fund the accounts. Amid growing concerns about AI job loss and the wealth gap, should other leaders promote this product, too? A few things to consider:

A way to promote financial wellness: Saddled with debt, stagnant wages and rising home costs—and tools that enable impulse investing—younger investors gravitate towards risky bets. “We could afford a house at 27 or 28. These kids can’t, so they look to quick‑buck flips, and that’s just not how markets work,” says Bill Capuzzi, CEO of Apex Fintech Solutions, which runs the infrastructure for many investing apps, reaching 41 million consumers. While the typical age for first-time home buyers has risen to 40, Gen Z is saving earlier for retirement. The Trump accounts could show the next generation of parents (and their kids) the power of prudent investing early on. Said Capuzzi: “Take this $1,000, don’t touch it, watch it compound over 18 years and learn how the markets really work.” (With parental contributions, Trump Account holders could have $270,000 by the age of 18.)

A family-friendly signal to talent: Some companies offer scholarships for employees’ children, but that can turn out to be a tax headache or source of resentment. Turns out it’s also not so easy to do a company match on Trump Accounts, which enable employers to deposit up to $2,500 into an account for each employee’s eligible child. Seventy percent of employers polled by Plan Sponsor Council of America last year said they didn’t plan to participate, citing the administrative burden, concerns about favoritism and lack of clarity around implementation. (Some already contribute to state 529 accounts.) BNY CEO Robin Vince signed on in December, praising the accounts as “a head start” for employees’ children. When I asked another leader yesterday about their plans, they waved away the topic, saying “we’re focused on improving what we already have.”

Story Continues

A long-term bet: Four million children have already signed up, according to IRS figures. The Dells plan to put $250 in accounts for 25 million kids, with Michael Dell telling me he hopes it “inspires children to want to learn more about compound interest.” I hope so, too. But any account bearing the name of a sitting president does carry another risk. As US Bank CEO Gunjan Kedia noted when I asked her about it yesterday: “Whether this survives after three years, the next Congress will have a point of view on that.” Indeed. With national debt topping $39 trillion, future presidents may decide to deploy that capital in a different way.

Contact CEO Daily via Diane Brady at diane.brady@fortune.com

This story was originally featured on Fortune.com

title: H&R Block CEO shares the deeply human fear that separates middle managers from those destined for the C-suite
content:
Corporate America has long been drawn to the self-made executive narrative. But Curtis Campbell, the new CEO of H&R Block, tells his version with a realism that gives it unusual credibility.

Campbell, who grew up in a small Southern town and became the first in his family to attend college, traces his rise to a combination of luck, tenacity, and strong mentorship. The answer sounds straightforward, yet it reveals a view of leadership grounded in endurance and self-awareness rather than corporate mythology.

Campbell readily concedes that intellect alone does not determine who reaches the corner office. As he says, “There are a lot of people in that room that are much smarter than I will ever be.” Career progression, he says, emerges through persistence, learning, and a willingness to seek guidance before it arrives on its own.

His advice to younger professionals reflects that belief. He encourages them to identify colleagues with specific strengths and ask for their time. Someone who excels in presentations, leadership, or communication may offer lessons that are more valuable than a formal development program. “If you find somebody who has a superpower, they’ll love to talk to you about it,” he says. In his view, those conversations can help ambitious professionals recognize patterns in how strong performers operate and apply those lessons to their own careers.

Campbell is also describing a workplace reality in which deeply human feelings, including fear of rejection, intimidation, and reserve, can quietly shape a career. Those impulses are familiar to almost everyone, yet they can still become barriers to advancement when they keep capable people from pursuing the conversations and relationships that might move them forward. “That’s what makes a difference between somebody being caught in mid-level management and getting to higher levels of the organization,” says Campbell.

He is equally clear that visibility carries weight only when it rests on strong execution. Performance comes first, and reputation follows the quality of the work. “You can’t put yourself out there and expect people to take a meeting if you don’t perform well,” he says. His standard is exacting and elegantly simple. Whatever your job may be, do it exceptionally well.

That ethos becomes more vivid when he describes the examples that shaped it. Campbell points to his mother, who worked as a janitor, and says the principle applied to her work just as surely as it applied to his own. Excellence is not situational, he says, nor is it tied to title. He carried that same standard into his early years as an engineer, where he distinguished himself through the quality of his work before others took his larger ambitions seriously.

Story Continues

In the end, Campbell’s ascent story rests on a simple hierarchy that places performance, persistence, and earned credibility first. “My mom was a janitor. Be the best janitor on the planet. I was an engineer. I had to be the best engineer in the company,” he says. “If you can do those things, people will take your meeting.”

This story was originally featured on Fortune.com

title: Tax credits vs. deductions: What you need to know before filing
content:
Geltrude & Company founder and CEO Dan Geltrude chats with Yahoo Finance's Josh Lipton about little-known deductions that everyone should know about before the April 15 tax filing deadline.

Video Transcript

00:00 Speaker A

The tax filing deadline is almost here. So before you sign that form, want to make sure you know about all the possible deductions you could be taking. Joining me now is part of our write it off or nice try segment presented by H&R Block. It's Dan Geltrude, the founder and CEO of Geltrude and Company. Let's start here, Dan. Uh, simple. What's the difference? Walk us through this between a tax deduction and a credit.

00:27 Dan Geltrud

So, a tax deduction lowers your income. A tax credit actually lowers your tax bill dollar for dollar. So, for example, if you have $100 tax deduction, you lower your income by $100, but if you have $100 tax credit, you actually lower the tax by $100. So, a tax credit is much better than a tax deduction.

00:54 Speaker A

A big headline from last year, Dan, was the boost to the assault reduction cap. How do taxpayers, you know, they're listening right now. How do they know, Dan, if they should be taking these salt deductions?

01:07 Dan Geltrud

Well, this was a real political hot potato, right? So, yes, the salt the salt deduction was raised to $40,000. That's up from 10,000. Now, how do you know you qualify? Well, you have to be able to itemize your deductions to be able to qualify for that. Because remember, you get the benefit of the higher of your standard deduction versus your itemized deductions. So the salt deduction falls into itemizing. So that's one thing to keep in mind. And again, that cap is up to $40,000 for tax year 2025.

01:42 Speaker A

So just to pull on that thread down, what kind of things can be itemized for salt deductions?

01:47 Dan Geltrud

So, salt stands for state and local taxes. So, here's how it works. That covers property taxes and your state income tax or sales tax. So it's one or the other related to your state income tax or the amount of sales tax that you paid within that tax year.

02:08 Speaker A

A lot of folks, Dan, listening right now, they may not know they can write off student loan payments. Um, what walk us through how that works?

02:16 Dan Geltrud

So what's deductible related to student loans, and this is important to keep in mind, it's only the interest on those payments, right? It is not the principle. So, uh, it's not as big of a deduction as you may think, and you also have income limits that apply. So, yes, it's there. It's a good thing, but it's it may not be as much as you think it is based upon how much you paid versus what portion of that was interest.

02:46 Speaker A

For sports betters out there, I'm sure we got playing listening right now, Dan, can gambling losses offer a tax break?

02:55 Dan Geltrud

Uh actually, they can. Uh so the way the rule is right now in terms of for your 2025 tax year, you get to offset your gambling wins and your gambling losses. So, in effect, you can zero your gambling wins out. However, going into next year, or or meaning next tax year, meaning 2026, that gets limited to only 90% of your losses are allowed. So you could actually end up with a situation where you've made no money, but you still owe tax.

03:26 Speaker A

What about dividends that are reinvested, Dan? Can you write that off?

03:31 Dan Geltrud

Well, you know, the big question there is is related to whether the reinvestment of dividends can be deducted. So, reinvested dividends are not deductible. Now, people think, well, I didn't get any cash, so why should I have to pay any tax on it? Well, they're still considered earnings and what they do is they increase so you pay tax on those reinvested dividends, but what they do do is they increase your cost basis so that when you sell those investment investments, it does lower your capital gains.

04:02 Speaker A

Dan, as always, great to have you on the show. Thank you.
